title: Prepositions Complete
subtitle: Mastering In, On, At for Time and Place, Movement, Position, and Prepositional Phrases
overview: Prepositions are small but powerful words that show relationships between nouns or pronouns and other elements in a sentence. They indicate position, direction, time, manner, or logical relationships. This comprehensive module covers the complete spectrum of prepositional usage: time prepositions (in, on, at for dates, times, periods), place prepositions (in, on, at for locations), movement prepositions (to, from, into, across), positional prepositions (above, below, beside, between), and common prepositional phrases. Mastery of prepositions is essential for accurate English communication and is frequently tested in competitive examinations such as SSC, Banking, Railways, UPSC, and other government recruitment tests. This module addresses common errors made by native Hindi speakers and provides systematic practice to develop intuitive prepositional accuracy.

cards.title: What is a Preposition?
cards.definition: A preposition is a word placed before a noun or pronoun to show its relationship with another word in the sentence. Prepositions typically indicate location (where something is), direction (where something is going), time (when something happens), manner (how something happens), or logical relationships (reason, purpose, possession). Common prepositions include in, on, at, to, from, with, by, for, of, about, above, below, beside, between, among, through, during, since, until, and many others. The noun or pronoun following the preposition is called the object of the preposition, and together they form a prepositional phrase.
rules: Prepositions always come before a noun or pronoun (their object)
The preposition and its object form a prepositional phrase
Prepositions show relationships of time, place, direction, or manner
Common prepositions include in, on, at, to, from, by, with, for, of, about
The choice of preposition often depends on context and cannot always be translated literally from other languages

correct.text: The books are on the table.
correct.reason: Preposition 'on' shows the position relationship between books and table

correct.text: She arrived at nine o'clock.
correct.reason: Preposition 'at' indicates the specific time of arrival

correct.text: He walked to the station.
correct.reason: Preposition 'to' shows the direction of movement

correct.text: We have lived in Mumbai since 2020.
correct.reason: Prepositions 'in' (place) and 'since' (starting point in time) show relationships

correct.text: The cat jumped from the roof.
correct.reason: Preposition 'from' indicates the starting point of movement

incorrect.text: The books are in the table.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - books rest 'on' a flat surface, not 'in' (which implies enclosure)

incorrect.text: She arrived in nine o'clock.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'at' for specific clock times, not 'in'

incorrect.text: He walked in the station.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'to' for destination/direction, not 'in' (which implies being inside)

incorrect.text: We have lived in Mumbai from 2020.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'since' for starting point with present perfect, not 'from'

incorrect.text: The cat jumped of the roof.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'from' for starting point of movement, not 'of' (possession)

cards.title: Time Prepositions: In, On, At
cards.definition: The prepositions in, on, and at are used to indicate time, but each has specific usage rules. Use 'in' for longer periods (months, years, decades, centuries, seasons, parts of day), 'on' for specific days and dates, and 'at' for precise times on the clock and specific moments. The choice depends on the specificity of the time reference: 'in' is the most general (larger time blocks), 'on' is more specific (particular days), and 'at' is the most precise (exact moments or clock times).
rules: Use 'in' for months (in January), years (in 2024), seasons (in summer), parts of day (in the morning, but 'at night')
Use 'on' for days of the week (on Monday), specific dates (on 15th August), special days (on Independence Day)
Use 'at' for specific clock times (at 9 o'clock, at noon, at midnight), meal times (at breakfast, at lunch), and 'at night'
Exception: We say 'at night' (not in the night) but 'in the morning/afternoon/evening'
Use 'in' for durations (in two weeks, in five minutes) meaning 'after this period'

correct.text: The examination begins in June.
correct.reason: Use 'in' for months - June is a longer time period

correct.text: Independence Day is celebrated on 15th August.
correct.reason: Use 'on' for specific dates - 15th August is a particular day

correct.text: The train departs at 9:30 in the morning.
correct.reason: Use 'at' for exact clock time (9:30) and 'in' for part of day (morning)

correct.text: We lived in Delhi in 2018.
correct.reason: Use 'in' for years - 2018 is a longer time period

correct.text: The shops are closed at night.
correct.reason: Exception: use 'at night', not 'in the night'

incorrect.text: The examination begins on June.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'in' for months, not 'on'

incorrect.text: Independence Day is celebrated in 15th August.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'on' for specific dates, not 'in'

incorrect.text: The train departs in 9:30.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'at' for clock times, not 'in'

incorrect.text: We lived in Delhi on 2018.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'in' for years, not 'on'

incorrect.text: The shops are closed in night.
incorrect.reason: Exception error - must say 'at night', not 'in night' (article missing too)

cards.title: Place Prepositions: In, On, At
cards.definition: The same prepositions (in, on, at) are used for place and location, but with different logic than time. Use 'in' for enclosed spaces or larger areas (countries, cities, rooms), 'on' for surfaces and lines (streets, floors, pages), and 'at' for specific points or locations (addresses, buildings as points, events). Think of it this way: 'in' suggests being inside or surrounded by something, 'on' suggests contact with a surface, and 'at' treats the location as a point rather than an area.
rules: Use 'in' for countries, cities, towns, rooms, buildings (when inside), enclosed spaces (in the box, in the car)
Use 'on' for surfaces (on the table, on the floor), streets (on Park Street), floors of buildings (on the third floor)
Use 'at' for specific addresses (at 23 Park Street), buildings as points (at the station, at the office), events (at the party)
Use 'in' when you mean inside the building, 'at' when you mean the location as a point
Transportation: 'in' for cars/taxis (enclosed), 'on' for buses/trains/planes (you stand/sit on their surface)

correct.text: She lives in Mumbai.
correct.reason: Use 'in' for cities - Mumbai is a larger area

correct.text: The office is on the fifth floor.
We shall meet at the railway station.
correct.reason: Use 'at' for specific locations treated as points - station is a meeting point

correct.text: The books are on the shelf.
correct.reason: Use 'on' for surfaces - shelf is a flat surface

correct.text: He is in the car waiting for us.
correct.reason: Use 'in' for enclosed vehicles like cars

incorrect.text: She lives at Mumbai.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'in' for cities, not 'at' (unless you mean the airport: at Mumbai airport)

incorrect.text: The office is in the fifth floor.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'on' for floors, not 'in'

incorrect.text: We shall meet in the railway station.
incorrect.reason: Usually use 'at' for stations as meeting points; 'in' implies being inside the building

incorrect.text: The books are in the shelf.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - books rest 'on' the shelf surface, not enclosed 'in' it

incorrect.text: He is on the car waiting for us.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'in' for cars (enclosed space), not 'on' (unless sitting on the roof)

cards.title: Movement Prepositions
cards.definition: Movement prepositions indicate direction or the path of motion. Common movement prepositions include 'to' (destination), 'from' (starting point), 'into' (entering an enclosed space), 'out of' (exiting), 'onto' (moving to a surface), 'off' (leaving a surface), 'through' (passing via), 'across' (from one side to another), 'along' (following a line), 'towards/toward' (in the direction of), 'past' (going beyond), 'around/round' (encircling or surrounding), 'up' (ascending), 'down' (descending). The choice of movement preposition depends on the type of motion and the nature of the destination or path.
rules: Use 'to' for destinations: go to school, travel to Delhi, move to a new house
Use 'from' for starting points: come from Mumbai, travel from Delhi to Kolkata
Use 'into' when entering an enclosed space: walk into the room, jump into the water
Use 'onto' when moving to a surface: climb onto the roof, step onto the platform
Use 'through' for movement within a space: walk through the park, drive through the tunnel

correct.text: She goes to school every day.
correct.reason: Use 'to' to indicate destination - school is where she is going

correct.text: He jumped into the swimming pool.
correct.reason: Use 'into' for entering an enclosed space - pool contains water

correct.text: The train travelled from Delhi to Mumbai.
correct.reason: Use 'from' for starting point and 'to' for destination

correct.text: We walked through the garden to reach the gate.
correct.reason: Use 'through' for movement within a space - moving inside the garden area

correct.text: The cat climbed onto the roof.
correct.reason: Use 'onto' for moving to a surface - roof is a flat surface

incorrect.text: She goes in school every day.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'to' for destination, not 'in' (you can be 'in' school once there)

incorrect.text: He jumped in the swimming pool.
incorrect.reason: Less precise - use 'into' to show movement of entering, not static 'in'

incorrect.text: The train travelled of Delhi to Mumbai.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'from' for starting point, not 'of'

incorrect.text: We walked in the garden to reach the gate.
incorrect.reason: Ambiguous - 'in' suggests being inside; use 'through' to show passage/movement

incorrect.text: The cat climbed on the roof.
incorrect.reason: Less precise - use 'onto' to show movement to the surface, not static 'on'

cards.title: Positional Prepositions
cards.definition: Positional prepositions describe the static position or location of one object in relation to another. These include 'above' (higher than, but not touching), 'below' (lower than), 'over' (directly above, may be touching or covering), 'under' (directly below, may be touching), 'beside/next to' (at the side of), 'between' (in the space separating two things), 'among' (surrounded by more than two), 'in front of' (facing), 'behind' (at the back of), 'inside' (within an enclosed space), 'outside' (external to), 'near' (close to), 'opposite' (facing directly across from), 'against' (touching and supported by).
rules: Use 'above' when something is higher without touching: clouds above the mountain
Use 'over' when something is directly above or covering: bridge over the river, blanket over the bed
Use 'below' for lower position without contact: valley below the peak
Use 'under' for directly beneath or covered by: cat under the table, under the blanket
Use 'between' for two objects, 'among' for more than two: between two students, among the crowd

correct.text: The clock hangs above the door.
correct.reason: Use 'above' for higher position without direct contact

correct.text: The bridge passes over the river.
correct.reason: Use 'over' when something spans across or covers from above

correct.text: She sat between her two friends.
correct.reason: Use 'between' for position in the space separating two people

correct.text: The agreement was made among all the partners.
correct.reason: Use 'among' for more than two people or things

correct.text: The cat is hiding under the bed.
correct.reason: Use 'under' for position directly beneath something

incorrect.text: The clock hangs over the door.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - 'over' suggests covering or directly above; use 'above' for higher position

incorrect.text: The bridge passes above the river.
incorrect.reason: Less natural - 'over' is preferred when something spans across

incorrect.text: She sat among her two friends.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'between' for exactly two, not 'among'

incorrect.text: The agreement was made between all the partners.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'among' for more than two, not 'between'

incorrect.text: The cat is hiding below the bed.
incorrect.reason: Unnatural - use 'under' for directly beneath with possible contact, not 'below'

cards.title: Prepositional Phrases
cards.definition: A prepositional phrase is a group of words consisting of a preposition, its object (a noun or pronoun), and any modifiers of that object. Prepositional phrases function as adjectives (describing nouns) or adverbs (describing verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs) in sentences. They add detail about time, place, manner, cause, or purpose. Many prepositional phrases are fixed expressions or idioms that must be learned as complete units: 'in spite of', 'on behalf of', 'in accordance with', 'with regard to', 'by means of', 'in addition to', 'on account of', 'for the sake of', 'in favour of', 'at the expense of'.
rules: A prepositional phrase begins with a preposition and ends with its object (noun/pronoun)
Prepositional phrases can modify nouns (adjectival) or verbs/adjectives/adverbs (adverbial)
Many prepositional phrases are fixed expressions and must be memorised as units
The object of the preposition is always in objective case: with him (not with he)
Multiple prepositional phrases can appear in one sentence

correct.text: He succeeded in spite of many difficulties.
correct.reason: Fixed phrase 'in spite of' means 'despite' - correct usage

correct.text: She spoke on behalf of the entire team.
correct.reason: Fixed phrase 'on behalf of' means 'representing' - correct usage

correct.text: The decision was made in accordance with the rules.
correct.reason: Fixed phrase 'in accordance with' means 'following' - correct usage

correct.text: He stayed at home on account of bad weather.
correct.reason: Fixed phrase 'on account of' means 'because of' - correct usage

correct.text: The book on the table belongs to me.
correct.reason: Prepositional phrase 'on the table' modifies the noun 'book' (adjectival)

incorrect.text: He succeeded despite of many difficulties.
incorrect.reason: Wrong form - either use 'despite' alone or 'in spite of', not 'despite of'

incorrect.text: She spoke in behalf of the entire team.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - the fixed phrase is 'on behalf of', not 'in behalf of'

incorrect.text: The decision was made in accordance to the rules.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - the fixed phrase is 'in accordance with', not 'to'

incorrect.text: He stayed at home in account of bad weather.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - the fixed phrase is 'on account of', not 'in account of'

incorrect.text: The book in the table belongs to me.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - books rest 'on' a surface, not 'in' (enclosed)

cards.title: Prepositions After Verbs
cards.definition: Many verbs require specific prepositions to complete their meaning. These verb-preposition combinations (also called prepositional verbs) must be learned as fixed patterns because the preposition choice is often idiomatic and cannot be predicted by logic or translation. Common patterns include: agree with (someone), agree to (something), depend on, wait for, look at, listen to, think about/of, talk about/to, laugh at, arrive at (small places), arrive in (cities/countries), apologise for, apply for (job), apply to (institution), believe in, belong to, care about/for, complain about.
rules: Verb + preposition combinations are fixed and must be memorised
The preposition choice is often idiomatic and differs from literal translation
Some verbs take different prepositions with different meanings: agree with (person) vs agree to (proposal)
Using the wrong preposition changes the meaning or makes the sentence incorrect
These combinations appear frequently in competitive examinations

correct.text: I agree with you on this matter.
correct.reason: Use 'agree with' when agreeing with a person

correct.text: She agreed to the proposal.
correct.reason: Use 'agree to' when agreeing to accept something

correct.text: Success depends on hard work.
We are waiting for the results.
correct.reason: 'Wait for' is the correct verb-preposition combination

correct.text: They arrived in Delhi yesterday.
correct.reason: Use 'arrive in' for cities and countries

incorrect.text: I agree to you on this matter.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'agree with' for persons, not 'agree to'

incorrect.text: She agreed with the proposal.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'agree to' for things/proposals, not 'agree with'

incorrect.text: Success depends of hard work.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - must use 'depend on', not 'depend of'

incorrect.text: We are waiting the results.
incorrect.reason: Missing preposition - must use 'wait for', not just 'wait' (direct object)

incorrect.text: They arrived at Delhi yesterday.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'arrive in' for cities, 'arrive at' only for small specific places

cards.title: Prepositions After Adjectives
cards.definition: Many adjectives are followed by specific prepositions to complete their meaning. These adjective-preposition combinations are also fixed patterns. Common combinations include: afraid of, angry with (person)/about (situation), aware of, capable of, different from, famous for, fond of, good at, interested in, pleased with, proud of, responsible for, similar to, tired of, worried about, satisfied with, jealous of, suitable for, inferior to, superior to, preferable to, familiar with, grateful to/for.
rules: Adjective + preposition combinations are fixed expressions
Some adjectives take different prepositions for different meanings: angry with (person) vs angry about (situation)
The preposition completes the meaning of the adjective
These patterns must be memorised as they do not follow predictable rules
Using the wrong preposition is a common error in competitive exams

correct.text: She is afraid of darkness.
correct.reason: 'Afraid of' is the correct fixed combination

correct.text: He is good at mathematics.
correct.reason: 'Good at' is used for skills or subjects

correct.text: The students are interested in science.
Mumbai is famous for its film industry.
correct.reason: 'Famous for' indicates what something is known for

correct.text: I am satisfied with the results.

incorrect.text: She is afraid from darkness.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - must use 'afraid of', not 'afraid from'

incorrect.text: He is good in mathematics.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'good at' for skills, not 'good in'

incorrect.text: The students are interested for science.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - must use 'interested in', not 'interested for'

incorrect.text: Mumbai is famous with its film industry.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'famous for', not 'famous with'

incorrect.text: I am satisfied from the results.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - must use 'satisfied with', not 'satisfied from'

cards.title: Prepositions of Time: Special Cases
cards.definition: Beyond the basic in/on/at rules, several time prepositions have specific uses: 'since' (starting point continuing to now, used with present perfect), 'for' (duration of time), 'during' (throughout a period), 'by' (not later than, deadline), 'until/till' (up to a point in time), 'from...to' (time span), 'within' (before the end of a period), 'ago' (time before now, used with past simple), 'before' (earlier than), 'after' (later than). Choosing the correct time preposition depends on whether you are indicating a point in time, a duration, or a relationship between times.
rules: Use 'since' for starting point with present perfect: I have lived here since 2020
Use 'for' for duration with any tense: I lived there for five years
Use 'during' for when something happens within a period: during the meeting
Use 'by' for deadlines: finish by Friday (not later than Friday)
Use 'until/till' for continuation up to a point: wait until tomorrow

correct.text: She has worked here since January.
correct.reason: 'Since' indicates starting point with present perfect tense

correct.text: He studied for three hours.
The phone rang during the examination.
correct.reason: 'During' indicates when something happened within a period

correct.text: Please submit the assignment by Monday.
We shall wait until he arrives.
correct.reason: 'Until' indicates continuation up to a specific point

incorrect.text: She has worked here from January.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'since' with present perfect for starting point, not 'from'

incorrect.text: He studied since three hours.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'for' with duration, not 'since' (which needs a point in time)

incorrect.text: The phone rang while the examination.
incorrect.reason: Wrong word - use 'during' with nouns, 'while' with clauses (while we were taking the examination)

incorrect.text: Please submit the assignment until Monday.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'by' for deadlines, 'until' means you can submit any time up to Monday

incorrect.text: We shall wait by he arrives.
incorrect.reason: Wrong preposition - use 'until' for continuation to a point, 'by' is for deadlines with nouns

cards.title: Prepositions vs Adverbs
cards.definition: Some words can function as either prepositions or adverbs depending on whether they have an object. When followed by a noun or pronoun, the word is a preposition. When standing alone without an object, it is an adverb. Common words with dual functions include: about, above, across, after, along, before, behind, below, down, in, near, off, on, over, past, round, through, under, up. Understanding this distinction helps avoid errors, especially with phrasal verbs where the particle can be either a preposition or an adverb.
rules: A preposition must have an object (noun or pronoun following it)
An adverb stands alone and modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb
Test: If you can ask 'what?' or 'whom?' after the word and get an answer, it is a preposition
In phrasal verbs, the particle may be an adverb (no object) or preposition (with object)
The same word changes function based on whether an object follows

correct.text: She climbed up the stairs. (preposition)
correct.reason: 'Up' is followed by object 'stairs', so it functions as a preposition

correct.text: She climbed up. (adverb)
correct.reason: 'Up' has no object following it, so it functions as an adverb modifying 'climbed'

correct.text: He came in the room. (preposition)
correct.reason: 'In' is followed by object 'room', functioning as preposition

correct.text: He came in. (adverb)
correct.reason: 'In' stands alone without object, functioning as adverb

correct.text: Look at the picture. (preposition)
correct.reason: 'At' has object 'picture', so it is a preposition

incorrect.text: She climbed up to the stairs.
incorrect.reason: Redundant - use 'up the stairs' (preposition) or 'up to the door' (two prepositions), not 'up to the stairs'

incorrect.text: He came in into the room.
incorrect.reason: Redundant - use either 'came in' (adverb) or 'came into the room' (preposition), not both

incorrect.text: The book is in inside the drawer.
incorrect.reason: Redundant - use either 'in' or 'inside', not both together

incorrect.text: He fell down from the stairs.
incorrect.reason: Redundant - use 'fell down the stairs' or 'fell from the stairs', not 'down from'

incorrect.text: Come on upon the stage.
incorrect.reason: Redundant - use 'come onto the stage' or 'come upon' (different meaning: discover), not 'on upon'

cards.title: Unnecessary Prepositions
cards.definition: Many errors occur when prepositions are used where they are not needed. Common cases include: after certain verbs that take direct objects without prepositions (discuss, marry, reach, enter, approach, order, resemble, comprise), before infinitives after certain verbs (want to go, not want for to go), with question words in indirect questions (I asked where he lived, not where did he live at), and in certain time expressions (next week, last year, every day - no preposition needed). Omitting unnecessary prepositions makes sentences clearer and more formal.
rules: Verbs like discuss, marry, reach, enter, approach take direct objects (no preposition)
Do not use prepositions before 'home' as an adverb: go home, reach home (not to home)
Time expressions like next/last/this/every + time word need no preposition
Question words in indirect questions do not take prepositions at the end in formal English
Avoid double prepositions: off of, outside of (use off, outside alone)

correct.text: We discussed the problem yesterday.
She married a doctor.
They reached Delhi on time.
He goes home every evening.
correct.reason: No preposition needed before 'home' when used as adverb

correct.text: We shall meet next Monday.
correct.reason: No preposition needed with 'next' + time word

incorrect.text: We discussed about the problem yesterday.
incorrect.reason: Unnecessary preposition - 'discuss' takes direct object, not 'discuss about'

incorrect.text: She married with a doctor.
incorrect.reason: Unnecessary preposition - use 'marry someone', not 'marry with someone'

incorrect.text: They reached to Delhi on time.
incorrect.reason: Unnecessary preposition - 'reach' takes direct object, not 'reach to'

incorrect.text: He goes to home every evening.
incorrect.reason: Unnecessary preposition - when 'home' is an adverb (not 'his home'), no 'to' needed

incorrect.text: We shall meet on next Monday.
incorrect.reason: Unnecessary preposition - 'next Monday' needs no preposition

cards.title: Hindi 'Mein' Translated as 'In' Everywhere
cards.definition: Hindi speakers often translate 'mein' (में) directly as 'in' in all contexts, leading to errors. Hindi uses one word 'mein' for many English prepositions including in, at, on, into. English requires different prepositions based on the specific context: 'at' for specific points (station, door, specific times), 'on' for surfaces and days, 'in' for enclosed spaces and longer time periods, 'into' for movement of entering. The Hindi single-word approach does not work in English, where preposition choice is more precise and context-dependent.
rules: Hindi 'mein' becomes 'at' for specific locations: station par (at the station), not in the station
Hindi 'mein' becomes 'on' for surfaces and days: table par (on the table), Monday ko (on Monday)
Hindi 'mein' stays 'in' for cities, enclosed spaces: Mumbai mein (in Mumbai), room mein (in the room)
Use 'into' when showing movement of entering, not static 'in'
Context determines which English preposition matches Hindi 'mein'

correct.text: I shall meet you at the station.
correct.reason: Station is a specific meeting point, not an enclosed space - use 'at'

correct.text: The books are on the table.
correct.reason: Table is a surface - use 'on', not 'in'

correct.text: He jumped into the pool.
correct.reason: Entering an enclosed space - use 'into' to show movement

correct.text: She works at the university.
The examination is on Monday.

incorrect.text: I shall meet you in the station.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - Hindi 'station par' is 'at the station', not 'in' (unless you mean inside the building)

incorrect.text: The books are in the table.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - Hindi 'table par' is 'on the table', books cannot be inside a solid table

incorrect.text: He jumped in the pool.
incorrect.reason: Imprecise - use 'into' to show entering movement, not static 'in'

incorrect.text: She works in the university.
incorrect.reason: Unnatural - Hindi 'university mein' as workplace is 'at the university' (specific location)

incorrect.text: The examination is in Monday.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - Hindi 'Monday ko' is 'on Monday', use 'on' for days

cards.title: Wrong Preposition After 'Reach' and 'Discuss'
cards.definition: Hindi speakers often add unnecessary prepositions after verbs 'reach' and 'discuss' because Hindi equivalents use postpositions. In Hindi, you say 'pahunchna' followed by 'par/mein' (reach at/to/in), and 'charcha karna' followed by 'par' (discuss about). However, English verbs 'reach' and 'discuss' are transitive and take direct objects without any preposition. This is a major error pattern in competitive exams and must be corrected through conscious practice.
rules: Use 'reach' + direct object (no preposition): reach Delhi, reach home, reach the station
Use 'discuss' + direct object (no preposition): discuss the problem, discuss the matter
Never use 'reach to' or 'reach at' - these are always wrong
Never use 'discuss about' - this is always wrong
Similar verbs: enter (not enter into), approach (not approach to), order (not order for)

correct.text: We reached the airport on time.
correct.reason: 'Reach' takes direct object 'airport' without any preposition

correct.text: Let us discuss this matter tomorrow.
The train reached Mumbai at midnight.
correct.reason: Direct object 'Mumbai' after 'reach', no preposition needed

correct.text: They discussed the proposal in detail.
She reached home late yesterday.

incorrect.text: We reached to the airport on time.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - Hindi influence 'pahunchna par', but English 'reach' needs no preposition

incorrect.text: Let us discuss about this matter tomorrow.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - Hindi 'charcha karna par', but English 'discuss' needs no preposition

incorrect.text: The train reached at Mumbai at midnight.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - 'reach at' is always incorrect, use 'reach' + direct object

incorrect.text: They discussed on the proposal in detail.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - 'discuss on' is incorrect, use 'discuss' + direct object

incorrect.text: She reached at home late yesterday.
incorrect.reason: Double error - 'reach at' wrong, and 'home' as adverb needs no preposition anyway

cards.title: Confusion Between 'Since' and 'For' with Time
cards.definition: Hindi uses 'se' (से) for both starting point and duration, but English distinguishes between 'since' (starting point in time) and 'for' (duration/length of time). 'Since' is used with present perfect tense to show an action that started at a specific point and continues to now: since 2020, since Monday, since morning. 'For' indicates how long something has lasted: for three years, for two days, for a long time. Hindi speakers often confuse these because 'se' translates both, but English requires precise differentiation.
rules: Use 'since' + specific point in time: since 2020, since January, since Monday, since 9 o'clock
Use 'for' + duration/period: for three years, for five days, for two hours, for a long time
'Since' requires present perfect tense: I have lived here since 2020
'For' can be used with various tenses: I lived there for five years (past), I have lived here for five years (present perfect)
Test: If you can answer 'How long?', use 'for'. If you answer 'From when?', use 'since'

correct.text: I have lived in Mumbai since 2018.
She has been ill for three days.
We have known each other since childhood.
He studied French for two years.
They have been waiting since morning.

incorrect.text: I have lived in Mumbai for 2018.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - 2018 is a starting point, use 'since', not 'for' (Hindi 'se' misleads here)

incorrect.text: She has been ill since three days.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - 'three days' is duration, use 'for', not 'since'

incorrect.text: We have known each other for childhood.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - 'childhood' is starting point, use 'since', not 'for'

incorrect.text: He studied French since two years.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - 'two years' is duration, use 'for', not 'since'

incorrect.text: They have been waiting for morning.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - 'morning' is starting point (since morning began), use 'since', not 'for'

cards.title: Saying 'Marry With' Instead of 'Marry'
cards.definition: Hindi speakers often say 'marry with' because in Hindi the construction is 'shaadi karna + ke saath' (marry with). However, English verb 'marry' is transitive and takes a direct object without any preposition. You marry someone (direct object), not marry with someone. The related noun 'marriage' does use 'to': marriage to someone. Similarly, the passive form uses 'to': She is married to a doctor. But the active verb 'marry' never takes a preposition.
rules: Active verb 'marry' + direct object (no preposition): marry someone, marry a doctor
Passive 'be married' + 'to': be married to someone
Noun 'marriage' + 'to': marriage to someone
Never use 'marry with' - this is always wrong
Related error: 'get married' (no preposition) or 'get married to someone' (with preposition)

correct.text: She married a software engineer last year.
correct.reason: Active verb 'married' takes direct object 'engineer' without preposition

correct.text: He is married to my sister.
They got married in December.
correct.reason: 'Got married' without object needs no preposition

correct.text: Her marriage to him was unexpected.
Whom did she marry?

incorrect.text: She married with a software engineer last year.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - Hindi influence 'shaadi karna ke saath', but English 'marry' needs no preposition

incorrect.text: He is married with my sister.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - passive form requires 'married to', not 'married with'

incorrect.text: They got married with each other in December.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - 'got married' needs no preposition (or use 'got married to each other')

incorrect.text: Her marriage with him was unexpected.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - noun 'marriage' uses 'to', not 'with'

incorrect.text: With whom did she marry?
incorrect.reason: Wrong structure - should be 'Whom did she marry?' (no preposition with active verb)

cards.title: Using 'From' Instead of 'Than' in Comparisons
cards.definition: Hindi speakers often use 'different from' correctly but then incorrectly extend this pattern to comparisons, saying 'more from' or 'better from' because Hindi uses 'se' (से) for both 'from' and 'than'. In English, 'from' is used with 'different' (different from) but comparisons always use 'than': more than, better than, faster than, taller than. This is a common error in competitive exams. Additionally, 'different from' is standard British English (preferred in India), though 'different than' is used in American English.
rules: Use 'different from' (British English) or 'different than' (American English)
Use 'than' (not from) with all comparatives: more than, better than, faster than
Hindi 'se' becomes 'than' in comparisons, not 'from'
Never use 'more from', 'better from', 'superior from' - always use 'than'
Exception: 'other than' (meaning except) is correct

correct.text: Delhi is different from Mumbai.
She is more intelligent than her brother.
This book is better than that one.
Gold is superior to silver.
correct.reason: Special case: 'superior to', 'inferior to', 'senior to', 'junior to' use 'to', not 'than'

correct.text: He is taller than I am.

incorrect.text: She is more intelligent from her brother.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - Hindi 'se' in comparisons becomes 'than', not 'from'

incorrect.text: This book is better from that one.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - comparative 'better' must use 'than', never 'from'

incorrect.text: Gold is superior than silver.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - exception: 'superior' uses 'to', not 'than' (though it seems like a comparative)

incorrect.text: Delhi is different than Mumbai.
incorrect.reason: Acceptable in American English but non-standard in British/Indian English - prefer 'different from'

incorrect.text: He is taller from me.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - comparative 'taller' requires 'than', not 'from'

cards.title: Time Prepositions Practice
cards.definition: Practice selecting the correct preposition (in, on, at, since, for, during, by, until) based on time context. Remember: 'in' for longer periods, 'on' for days/dates, 'at' for specific times, 'since' for starting points with present perfect, 'for' for duration, 'during' for within a period, 'by' for deadlines, 'until' for continuation to a point.
rules: Identify whether the time reference is a point, period, or duration
Check the verb tense to determine if 'since' (present perfect) or 'for' is appropriate
Distinguish between 'by' (deadline) and 'until' (continuation)
Remember exceptions: 'at night', 'in the morning/afternoon/evening'
Parts of day use 'in', but night uses 'at'

correct.text: The results will be announced in July.
The meeting is scheduled on 15th June.
We shall leave at 8 o'clock in the morning.
She has worked here since 2019.
He slept for ten hours last night.
The phone rang during the lecture.
Please submit the form by Friday.
We shall wait until he returns.

incorrect.text: The results will be announced on July.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - months use 'in', not 'on'

incorrect.text: The meeting is scheduled in 15th June.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - specific dates use 'on', not 'in'

incorrect.text: We shall leave in 8 o'clock.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - clock times use 'at', not 'in'

incorrect.text: She has worked here from 2019.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - starting point with present perfect uses 'since', not 'from'

incorrect.text: He slept since ten hours last night.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - duration uses 'for', not 'since'

cards.title: Place Prepositions Practice
cards.definition: Practice choosing the correct preposition (in, on, at) for place and location. Think about whether the location is an enclosed space (in), a surface or line (on), or a specific point (at). Consider whether you are describing being inside something, resting on a surface, or treating a location as a point of reference.
rules: Use 'in' for countries, cities, rooms, enclosed spaces: in India, in the box, in the car
Use 'on' for surfaces, streets, floors: on the table, on Park Street, on the third floor
Use 'at' for specific points, addresses, buildings as locations: at 23 Park Street, at the station, at the party
Transportation: 'in' for cars/taxis (enclosed), 'on' for buses/trains/planes
Think: Are you inside it (in)? On its surface (on)? Treating it as a point (at)?

correct.text: She lives in Bangalore.
The shop is on MG Road.
We shall meet at the railway station.
The keys are on the desk.
He is in the car waiting for us.
She travelled on the train to Delhi.
The office is on the fifth floor.
There is a party at his house tonight.
correct.reason: House as event location (point) → 'at' (but 'in his house' if focusing on interior)

incorrect.text: She lives at Bangalore.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - cities use 'in', not 'at'

incorrect.text: The shop is at MG Road.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - streets use 'on', not 'at' (unless you mean at the junction/crossing)

incorrect.text: The keys are in the desk.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - keys rest on the surface, not inside (unless the desk has drawers and they are inside)

incorrect.text: He is on the car waiting for us.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - cars are enclosed, use 'in', not 'on' (unless sitting on the roof)

incorrect.text: She travelled in the train to Delhi.
incorrect.reason: Less common - prefer 'on the train' (standard British English)

cards.title: Movement Prepositions Practice
cards.definition: Practice using movement prepositions (to, from, into, out of, onto, off, through, across, along, towards, past, around) to indicate direction and path of motion. Focus on whether you are describing a destination (to), starting point (from), entering an enclosure (into), moving onto a surface (onto), or passing through or across something.
rules: Use 'to' for destinations: go to school, travel to Mumbai
Use 'from' for starting points: come from Delhi, travel from here
Use 'into' when entering an enclosed space: walk into the room, jump into water
Use 'onto' when moving to a surface: climb onto the roof, step onto the stage
Use 'through' when moving within a space: walk through the park, drive through the tunnel

correct.text: She goes to college every day.
He came from Mumbai yesterday.
The children ran into the playground.
She climbed onto the platform.
We walked through the garden.
The river flows across the valley.
correct.reason: From one side to the other → 'across'

correct.text: They drove past the monument.
We walked towards the temple.

incorrect.text: She goes in college every day.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - destination uses 'to', not 'in' (you can be 'in' college once there)

incorrect.text: He came of Mumbai yesterday.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - starting point uses 'from', not 'of'

incorrect.text: The children ran in the playground.
incorrect.reason: Imprecise - 'into' shows entering movement, 'in' is static

incorrect.text: She climbed on the platform.
incorrect.reason: Imprecise - 'onto' shows movement to surface, 'on' is static

incorrect.text: We walked in the garden.
incorrect.reason: Ambiguous - 'in' suggests being inside, 'through' shows passage

cards.title: Verb-Preposition Combinations Practice
cards.definition: Practice using fixed verb-preposition combinations. These must be memorised as units because the preposition choice is idiomatic. Focus on commonly confused pairs like agree with/to, wait for, depend on, arrive at/in, apologise for, apply for/to, listen to, look at, think about/of, and others.
rules: Memorise fixed combinations: agree with (person), agree to (thing), depend on, wait for
Arrive at (small places), arrive in (cities/countries)
Apologise for (mistake), apply for (job), apply to (institution)
Listen to (not listen), look at (not look), think about (consider), think of (opinion)
These combinations are tested frequently in competitive exams

correct.text: I agree with your opinion.
She agreed to the terms.
Success depends on hard work.
We are waiting for the bus.
They arrived at the station early.
We arrived in London yesterday.
He apologised for being late.
She applied for the job last week.

incorrect.text: I agree to your opinion.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - use 'agree with' for persons/opinions, not 'agree to'

incorrect.text: She agreed with the terms.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - use 'agree to' for things/proposals, not 'agree with'

incorrect.text: Success depends of hard work.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - must use 'depend on', not 'depend of'

incorrect.text: We are waiting the bus.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - must use 'wait for', not direct object

incorrect.text: They arrived in the station early.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - use 'arrive at' for small places, not 'arrive in'

cards.title: Adjective-Preposition Combinations Practice
cards.definition: Practice using fixed adjective-preposition combinations. These patterns are idiomatic and must be memorised. Focus on commonly tested combinations: afraid of, good at, interested in, different from, famous for, proud of, satisfied with, worried about, angry with/about, responsible for, similar to, familiar with.
rules: Memorise fixed combinations: afraid of, good at, interested in, famous for
Different from (British), proud of, satisfied with, worried about
Angry with (person), angry about (situation)
Responsible for, similar to, familiar with, capable of
These are frequently tested in error spotting questions

correct.text: She is afraid of darkness.
He is good at mathematics.
They are interested in music.
Mumbai is famous for its film industry.
She is proud of her achievements.
I am satisfied with the results.
He is worried about the examination.
This design is similar to the previous one.

incorrect.text: She is afraid from darkness.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - must use 'afraid of', not 'afraid from'

incorrect.text: He is good in mathematics.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - use 'good at' for skills, not 'good in'

incorrect.text: They are interested for music.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - must use 'interested in', not 'interested for'

incorrect.text: Mumbai is famous with its film industry.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - must use 'famous for', not 'famous with'

incorrect.text: She is proud with her achievements.
incorrect.reason: Wrong - must use 'proud of', not 'proud with'

sections.title: Quick Revision Points
keyPoints: Time: 'in' for months/years/seasons, 'on' for days/dates, 'at' for clock times and 'at night'
Place: 'in' for enclosed spaces/cities, 'on' for surfaces/streets/floors, 'at' for specific points/addresses
Movement: 'to' for destination, 'from' for starting point, 'into' for entering, 'onto' for moving to surface
Since vs For: 'since' for starting point with present perfect (since 2020), 'for' for duration (for three years)
Common verb combinations: discuss (no preposition), reach (no preposition), marry (no preposition), wait for, depend on, arrive at/in, agree with/to
Common adjective combinations: afraid of, good at, interested in, different from, famous for, proud of, satisfied with, worried about
Fixed phrases: in spite of, on behalf of, in accordance with, on account of, by means of, in addition to
Unnecessary prepositions: Do not use prepositions with discuss, marry, reach, enter, approach; no preposition before home (as adverb) or with next/last/this/every + time
Hindi errors: 'mein' is not always 'in' (can be at/on/into); never use 'reach to', 'discuss about', 'marry with', 'more from', 'better from'
Comparisons: Use 'than' (not from) with comparatives: more than, better than; Exception: superior to, inferior to, senior to, junior to use 'to'
